data.title: MFA Financial Under The Microscope: Baby Bonds, Preferred Stocks And Credit Quality
data.description:
Today, we take a look at MFA Financial (MFA). We will go deep into the company, its credit quality, as well as its financial instruments and their yields.

MFA's current market capitalization is close to $1.03 billion. The total assets at the end of 2025 were around $13.04 billion, and the total debt was approximately $10.93 billion. The baby bonds represent $190 million, and the nominal value of the preferred stocks is approximately $475 million. The most significant portion of MFA's investment portfolio is close to 43% in Non-QM Loans and 27% in Agency MBS.

The recourse leverage is 2.5x. The key metric in evaluating mREITs is recourse leverage. At 2.5x, MFA is significantly more conservative than the average US bank, which typically operates at around 6x.

Baby Bonds

MFA has two baby bonds: (MFAN) and (MFAO):

Both are currently trading above par. You can see for yourself the difference in baby bond yields to worst from our last article here. Currently, MFAN offers a yield to worst of around 6.55%, and MFAO offers a yield to worst of around 5.78%, while in our last article, they were offering over 9%. The call date of MFAN has passed, while the call date of MFAO is 08/15/2026. Both bonds have ~3 years to maturity with maturity dates MFAN: 02/15/2029 and MFAO: 08/15/2029. In the next chart are shown the potential yields to worst and prices of both bonds:

MFAN:

MFAO:

Preferred Stocks

And below are shown the preferred stocks of the company:

(MFA.PR.C) with a floating rate and (MFA.PR.B) with a fixed rate. Both stocks are trading below par at the time of writing. And both are callable - their call dates have passed. The fixed-rate MFA.PR.B offers a yield to worst of around 9.23% - a competitive yield even compared to other fixed-rate preferred stocks that trade below par and are callable in the mortgage sector.

Here you can see the YTW vs. prices of MFA.PR.B chart:

At the same time, MFA.PR.C offers an attractive opportunity for investors as it switched from a fixed to a floating dividend in March 2025 with a significant spread of over 5.6% over SOFR. At the current market price below par, the instrument provides a yield to worst of around 10.17%, combining high current income with the potential for capital gains upon a return to parity.

Credit Quality

To assess the credit quality of MFA, we use several key metrics, which you can view below.

The market capitalization of cоmmon plus prеferred stock divided by the par value of the company's unsеcured bonds to dеtermine the "baby bond buffer." MFA has about 7.93 times as much common and preferred stock as bonds.

The dividеnds pаid on commоn and prefеrred stock are dividеd by the intеrest that will bе pаid on the bаby bоnds. Hеre, the ratio is close to 10.51.

The company's tоtal assets dividеd by the mаrket cаpitalization of cоmmon and prefеrred stоck givеs an idеa of ​​hоw indеbted the cоmpany is in compаrison. Here, the ratio is close to 8.66.

Conclusion

MFA demonstrates solid fundamentals for an mREIT, with a diversified portfolio centered on Non-QM loans and Agency MBS and a moderate level of leverage. The baby bonds currently offer yields to worst of around 6%, while the preferred stocks offer yields in excess of 9% and 10%, and a credit buffer of over 7.93 provides reasonable confidence in the company’s ability to service its debt. Even if you decide to invest in the fixed-rate preferred stock that looks the most attractive with a yield of over 9% and a price below par, it is good to know that 10%+ is not such a "safe" yield to worst. Such an investment requires active monitoring and a willingness to realize a loss if necessary if the fundamental picture changes significantly.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market's Plunge Offers Investors A Discount (Upgrade)
data.description:
The last article that I wrote about Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM), was published back in July of last year. In that article, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. Even though I acknowledged that the company was achieving impressive growth when it came to revenue, profits, and store count, I argued that shares were priced at levels that were difficult to justify. I wouldn't go so far as to take a bearish stance on it. But looking back, I probably should have. Shares have plummeted 53.2% since that article came out. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 4.5%. This plunge, it seems, has been the result of slowing growth, combined with the fact that shares were not cheap at that time. However, I would argue that the market has overreacted to this. Based on the data that's currently available, with management forecasting continued growth on the top line this year and with shares trading at levels that are attractively priced, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ makes a lot of sense here.

Even though I am making the decision to upgrade the company right now, it is possible that my mind could change as new data comes in. Interestingly enough, that should not be too far off into the future. Management has made clear to investors that, on April 29th, the company will announce financial results for the first quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. Revenue is expected to rise, but there is the belief among analysts that overall profits will worsen. Although this would be disappointing in and of itself, guidance provided by management for 2026 in its entirety suggests that results for the year won't really be any different than they were in 2025.

Shares Are Looking Tasty

Odds are, you already know what Sprouts Farmers Market is and what it does. But if you don't, it's helpful to know that the business operates as a specialty natural and organic food retailer that has successfully carved out a nice niche for itself in recent years. Historically, the institution prioritizes perishables over non-perishables. About 57% of revenue came from the perishables category last year. Unlike many retailers that are out there, the business is not afraid to innovate in interesting ways. This includes its own Sprouts-branded products that now account for just over 25% of the company's revenue. This is particularly intriguing since owning its own brands means that the business has greater operational control and that margins have a tendency to be higher than if the firm were to essentially act as a middleman, like it does for most of its revenue stream.

Over the last few years now, the firm has achieved some rather strong growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue for the institution jumped from $6.84 billion to $8.81 billion. A big driver behind the growth for the company has been a rise in the number of locations that it has in operation. Over the last three years, the business has gone from having 407 stores to having 477. But that's not all. Comparable store sales have also achieved rather remarkable results. In fact, over the last three years now, we have seen them increase quite a bit.

In 2025, as an example, they ended up coming in at 7.3% above what they were a year earlier. Management mentioned in their latest earnings call that the company continues to benefit in this regard from continued innovation and product differentiation. On the product side of things, management mentioned that throughout the year they introduced more than 7,000 new items to their stores, including more than 600 new products under its Sprouts brand. The rolling out of its loyalty program is also almost certainly proving to be a benefit to the business. As great as that is, in the most recent quarter on its own, the company saw comparable sales growth of just 1.6%. This is actually indicative of a meaningful deceleration in growth. We don't really have a lot of detail here. But management did mention that the macroeconomic environment remains uneven and that consumers are increasingly changing their preferences toward value-centric opportunities. Considering that natural and healthy options have a tendency to be more expensive than what else is out there, it's not shocking to see weakness in that regard.

As revenue for the company increases, profitability rises also. Net income more than doubled from $258.9 million to $523.7 million over the last three years. But that's not all, though. We saw operating cash flow expand from $465.1 million to $716 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $569.5 million to $871.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $528.2 million to $843.9 million.

The good news is that management expects revenue growth to at least continue throughout this year. They anticipate sales growth of between 4.5% and 6.5%. Unfortunately, pretty much all of this will come from an increase in the number of stores and operations. And that's because comparable store sales growth will range between being negative 1% and positive 1%. This will come from the addition of 40 new stores that management will allocate a large portion of its $280 million to $310 million of capital expenditures toward.

Achieving this target would likely translate to earnings per share, according to the company, of between $5.28 and $5.44. If the firm can achieve the midpoint of this range, that would mean net profits of $522.8 million. This would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of $869.7 million and EBITDA of $842.4 million. In the chart above, you can see what this would mean for the valuation of the business compared to how the company is valued using results from 2025. Honestly, I consider these to be very attractive multiples on their own. But the stock isn't cheap just on an absolute basis. It's also a bit near the cheaper end of the spectrum when it comes to other similar enterprises. In that same chart, you can see how the firm compares to five other similar enterprises. Only one of them ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. Two were cheaper when it came to the price-to-adjusted operating cash flow approach. And lastly, three of them were cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Overall, however, I would say that I am slightly bullish on the valuation of the company in its totality.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that, on April 29th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do expect continued revenue growth, with sales forecasted to rise from $2.24 billion to $2.33 billion. Again, the heavy lifting here should come from the rise in store count. Earnings per share, meanwhile, have been forecasted to come in at around $1.68. That would mean net profits of approximately $163.9 million. This compares to the $180 million in net profit that worked out to a profit per share of $1.83 that the company reported for the first quarter of 2025. The table below shows certain cash flow metrics for the company for the first quarter of 2025. If profitability does worsen, I would expect those to show some weakness also.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right this moment, I believe that the market has overreacted to what is clearly a case of slowing growth. The stock was priced at levels before that were difficult to justify, and we have seen play out exactly the kind of scenario that I feared. But the good news is that, given how cheap the stock is, I would say that it is most certainly worthy of an upgrade to a ‘buy’ right now.

data.title: Exchanges Profit Whether Markets Are Up Or Down, Part I: London Stock Exchange Group
data.description:
Why I Own Shares in This Exchange Now

It's all about trading volume. The entire world is in flux, with leadership in some nations prone to changing their minds, their positions, and even their public statements with reckless abandon. ("I never said that" is more and more popular these days.) Too much of that and, like George Carlin used to say, "I finally made up my mind, but now I can’t find where I put it."

This back-and-forth, on-and-off, creates huge volatility in the world's markets. Millions of people are selling one day, buying the next, and selling again on the third day. Who benefits? Certainly not those investors with no plan beyond today's headlines. Who really does benefit? Stock exchanges - and their owners, which are often much larger corporate entities.

But for the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEGY), there's yet another compelling reason to buy now.

The LSEG is the world's biggest venue for foreign exchange ("FX"). They dominate from their subsidiary multi-bank platform "FXall." If Nvidia (NVDA) needs to convert $3 billion from US dollars to Euros and $2 billion more to Chinese Yuan, FXall can quote from 50 different banks, all eager to offer the best deal in a second, and LSEG makes it happen.

When you're planning a holiday trip abroad and want to know the current exchange rate, this is most often the venue that provides the numbers.

When geopolitical tensions abound, currencies move. High movement equals high trading volume, and LSEG takes a small "toll" on every trade. Ka-ching, ka-ching.

FX is the largest and most liquid market in the world, trading $7 to $8 trillion dollars (every day).

I begin this series with a "stock exchange" that's only one part of this Leader of the Pack. That's why it is called the London Stock Exchange Group.

Don't Be Fooled By the Name "Exchange"

Why did I place the word “Group” in italics?

  1. Because that's the name of the organization that includes the LSE (London Stock Exchange) as a subsidiary.
  2. You need to know that the new LSE is not the stodgy old "boy’s club."

The steady, dependable LSE accounts for about a third of the LSE Group's revenue. While other exchanges may be looking backward to the Buttonwood Tree gathering, the LSE and the LSEG are charging ahead.

Where Does the Rest of LSEG’s Revenue Come From?

Do you remember the brilliant Avis ads from long ago that centered on the theme that, as an underdog to Hertz, they could not afford to treat you with disdain or disrespect like Avis claimed Hertz did? There was always the tag line in every print, TV, or radio ad: We’re No. 2. We Try Harder!

While the LSE itself is growing nicely, we have to say the NYSE and Nasdaq are not in danger of ceding their total trading numbers to the LSE just yet.

But there's another financial organization that's the biggest, some would say baddest, in the world, and LSEG is quietly gaining ground by "trying harder." That giant is...

Bloomberg. Yes, I know Bloomberg is not a stock exchange. That’s the point: Neither is LSEG; LSEG doesn't have to depend on a single primary income stream like Bloomberg does.

Though its roots were in the London Stock Exchange (more than 100 years before the NYSE), the LSEG is now the No. 2 provider of essential data used by brokerage firms and bankers’ trading departments, by financial journalists, by hedge funds, corporations, and individuals around the world. LSEG is enjoying the fruits of a strategic expansion at a disciplined velocity.

You will see data from LSEG, Bloomberg, and other smaller competitors across hundreds of financial publications and other journals. The one below is from a recent Wall Street Journal. Please note the source of the information.

Bloomberg is not sitting on its thumbs, of course. The old boxy Bloomberg terminal is gone (competition will do that). But you will still pay around $27,000 per year (with a mandatory two-year purchase) for a single terminal. For this, you get "Bloomberg Anywhere," meaning you can take your work home with you or on vacation. Oh, joy. As I once heard someone say about taking your digital life everywhere and all the time, "My bed is basically an auxiliary office with higher thread-count furniture."

Bloomberg has had great success in using this approach in the past. But the monolith is a closed loop. LSEG’s plan is to close that loop by stressing interoperability at a price about 25% lower. That might not be important to JPMorgan's (JPM) trading desks, but there are hundreds of other firms for whom this would be a serious consideration.

You see, LSEG has partnered with Microsoft (MSFT) in a deal where MSFT paid LSEG about $2 billion for a 4% stake in LSEG. What they expect in return is that LSEG will become a long-term customer for many of its "open-loop" and cloud services.

As part of the deal, LSEG committed to a minimum spend of $2.8 billion over the next 10 years on Microsoft’s cloud services (Azure, AI, Microsoft 365, Excel, Teams), and whatever else comes down the track so they can stay on the leading edge - while Bloomberg keeps the goodies "all in the family."

When I say LSEG is doing all this, US readers may be more familiar with the name "Refinitiv." Since the last time I was there, you couldn't escape Times Square without seeing the big electronic billboard touting the data research, analysis, and publication firm. Refinitiv is now LSEG.

All the News that Fits, LSEG Can Provide

While the primary focus for LSEG customers is market-moving data, the 30-year agreement covers a massive spectrum of content.

Looking ahead, LSEG realized that visual is the direction the world is heading. Data is good; data presented as graphs and charts is better; and having images—photography, videography, and more—is even better still. So they linked up with Thomson Reuters (TRI). This strategic partnership gives LSEG a 30-year lock on all Reuters news, graphics, photos, and related content for the financial community.

Unlike Bloomberg, LSEG users now receive the same real-time text alerts that are sent to newsrooms globally. This isn't restricted to "financial news." It includes breaking news, natural disasters, and human-interest stories. It includes politics, with extensive coverage of elections, policy shifts, and legislative changes, and it even intrudes on my area: Geopolitics, with in-depth coverage of global conflicts.

Of course, this deal includes Reuters’ award-winning visual journalism, not just charts and CEO headshots! "Newscasts" feature on the LSEG Workspace provide 24/7 live and on-demand video. This includes market analysis and field reports on major world events.

LSEG is growing in power because it's more than just a data provider. They can use this broader "World News" access to market themselves as more than just a data provider; LSEG is a "window to the world," successfully arguing that a professional cannot understand a stock price without also understanding the political, cultural, and social events surrounding it.

But Wait, There's More...

Let us not forget that LSEG also owns a very important stock exchange. That is easy to lose sight of since about 75% of LSEG’s total income is now subscriptions that are a gift that just keeps giving. That's why I think of it as DaaS -- Data as a Service.

The London Stock Exchange is no fuddy-duddy. Beating the NYSE to automated trading in 1986, this cultural and technological revolution saved London from becoming a second-class exchange and turned it into a global superpower instead.

The British like to call it "Wimbledonisation." The Wimbledon tennis tournament takes place on the outskirts of London, but British players haven't won in years. Still, it's Wimbledon. In the same way, the LSE not only allowed but also encouraged foreign investment.

Today, the LSE has fewer "big names" that list primarily or exclusively on the exchange than the NYSE or the nasdaq. (More on these as we continue this series.) But companies such as AstraZeneca (AZN), BAE Systems (BAESY), BP (BP), Diageo (DGE), and Shell (SHEL) are among the multinationals with a primary listing on the LSE. It's the lesser-known subsidiaries, well beyond the stock exchange, that inspire me to lead with my series on the world's best exchanges with the LSEG.

LSEGY created and manages the FTSE 100 and the Russell 2000, which means high income from ETFs and mutual funds.

Their London Clearing House is the world’s leading clearing house for interest rate swaps. Banks need this clearinghouse to manage their risk.

And LSEGY’s World-Check helps banks and corporations screen for financial crime, sanctions, and money laundering. Risk intelligence is essential in today's world. In fact, this subsidiary grew at nearly 14% in 2025. Quoting what LSEG says about this hot subsidiary: "Real-time risk intelligence. Built for a faster world."

What Kind of Numbers Are We Talking About?

For the fiscal year ending Dec. 31, 2025, LSEG reported a year with 50% EBITDA margins. I have not used FXall to convert from Pound Sterling to USD.

Income: £9,346 million reported, £8,986 million as adjusted*

Net Income: £1,180 million reported, £2,084 million as adjusted.

Operating Cash Flow: ~ £3,300 million

Free Cash Flow: £2.45 billion (a new record high)

Gross Margin: ~ 88%

Operating Margin: 22.8%

Net Margin: 12.6%

*This difference is primarily for the amortization of the Refinitiv purchase. This amount will be significantly smaller going forward.

[Today's exchange rate, courtesy of LSEG: £1 = $1.35, so £2.45 billion as of today = $3.32 billion.]

Are There Risks in Investing in LSEG?

Of course there are. These would include:

  • Competition from Japan, Europe, the US, and other stock, commodity, FX, and other exchanges around the world, many of which are also toying with expanding their offerings as they follow in LSEG's path.
  • LSEG's debt is high due to its acquisition of Refinitiv and its partnership with Thomson Reuters. Defrayed a good bit by the deal with Microsoft, LSEG cannot afford to rest on its laurels. To service the debt, they must keep those subscriptions rolling and get new ones.
  • If you buy in the US, you are buying an ADR. That means you're accepting the GBP/USD exchange rate. If the British Pound weakens, your LSEGY shares can lose value even if LSEGY's stock price in London remains unchanged. (On the other hand, if the USD keeps declining vs. the GBP, you will profit even if there is no movement in London.)
  • The London Stock Exchange is the largest stock market in Europe by market capitalization and number of listed international companies. It also dominates in mining and natural resources listings globally. That's a good place to be, but these markets can sometimes be fickle and volatile.

Is the London Stock Exchange Group a Compelling Buy?

Yes, I believe it is. LSEGY remains a powerhouse in FX, financial intelligence against fraud and other crimes, and the London Stock Exchange. But it's today becoming a financial data giant. This is how we need to view the company going forward. It's the un-Bloomberg as well, and has the newfound profits to show for it.

In addition to the key corporate metrics I described above, here are some comparisons of how LSEGY stacks up versus those I believe it will compete with going forward (excluding Bloomberg, which is private).

LSEG is no small fry. Its market cap and enterprise value are higher than those of both the Nasdaq and Deutsche Börse, the biggest competitor in Europe. They inherited many employees from the Refinitiv buyout, but they're being used to integrate and expand as part of the Microsoft deal.

I think the key issues to note here are LSEG's total cash per share, total debt to equity, book value per share, and long-term debt/total capital. These are important indicators of likely future performance. LSEG is not the outlier in any of these, but is near the best or solidly in the middle.

Here's where LSEG shines. It leads in net operating cash flow and cash from operations. Given the goals the company has set for itself, it needs to generate lots of cash. As far as their capital expenditures, they're not slowing down; they're in the middle here. And their plan is to buy more of its shares in 2026, just as they did in 2025.

Don't forget that LSE is a stock exchange. But definitely don't forget it's so much more.

Thank you for reading! Next up—I analyze Nasdaq (NDAQ).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alphabet Advances In AI, Positioned To Gain Leadership (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOG) is set to report q1’26 earnings on April 29, 2026, following an eventful quarter of closing the Wiz acquisition, deepening its partnership with Broadcom (AVGO) for custom silicon development, and incremental advancements in AI development. With the possibility of accelerating capital investments for developing its large-scale AI factories to power its AI models, I believe Alphabet may be in a position of strength among the hyperscalers in terms of AI development and adoption. Given the improved growth outlook and silicon deals secured in 2026, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for GOOG shares with a price target of $480/share at 19x eFY28 EV/EBITDA.

You can review my previous coverage of Alphabet here.

What to expect in Alphabet’s q1’26 Earnings Release

The leading headline that caught my attention after the close of q1’26 is in relation to Alphabet’s silicon chips in partnership with Broadcom. Accordingly, Broadcom announced a major partnership agreement with Anthropic (ANTHRO) to deliver 3.5GW of TPU capacity beginning in 2027. The deal is said to deliver nearly 1mm Google TPU v7p units. I believe that this deal secures Alphabet leadership as the custom silicon provider for AI development, particularly for external third-party customers. Beyond the deal with Anthropic, Alphabet is targeting neoclouds, or smaller hyperscalers, for investment and potential buildouts using Google TPUs.

A major area of focus for Alphabet is to develop a custom solution that provides the level of capabilities to go head-to-head with Nvidia’s (NVDA) GPUs while improving power density for AI inferencing. As part of its drive to deliver a functional stack, Alphabet recently announced a multi-year partnership with Intel Corp. (INTC) to collaborate in the development of Xeon CPUs and custom infrastructure processing units [IPUs] that can potentially integrate with Google TPUs. This could drive Alphabet’s positioning into developing a ready-to-deploy, rack-scale solution for smaller cloud providers and private enterprise data centers. Alphabet is also exploring a partnership with Marvell Technology (MRVL) to develop 2 new chip designs, diversifying from its dependency on Broadcom.

From a financial perspective, I believe revenue from the deal with Anthropic will begin to ramp up in e2h26 and accelerate in eq4’26.

In addition to success in expanding its custom silicon business, Alphabet is making strides in developing its AI models, releasing Gemma 4, built on Gemini 3, in early April 2026. Gemma 4 was designed for advanced reasoning, agentic workflows, code generation, and vision & audio as its most intelligent open models to date. The model was designed to run at the edge on laptop GPUs and developer workstations, an appealing feature given the increasing costs to rent cloud-hosted GPUs.

In addition to releasing Gemma 4, Alphabet released Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6, advancing its positioning in physical AI. Accordingly, the model was purpose-built with reasoning capabilities and the ability to Google Search to find information necessary to execute a physical task. My imagination might be wandering here, but this may mean that a robot could search for something as basic as how to change a vehicle’s engine oil, analyze a video or document, and undergo a task. The model enhances various abilities like spatial reasoning for precise object detection, relational logic for order of operations, motion reasoning to optimize physics, and constraint compliance to remain within reasonable parameters of a task. From a use case perspective, this could be a major advancement for robotics used in industrial manufacturing, health care, and other markets where precision is key. Borrowing from GlobalFoundries’ (GFS) outlook, physical AI is expected to become a growth trend near the end of the decade, positioning Alphabet to further enhance and scale its footprint with robotics manufacturers.

The last component is Alphabet’s completion of the acquisition of Wiz. Previously announced in March 2025, Alphabet acquired the cybersecurity company to integrate the platform into its cloud unit. Wiz’s security offerings can be exceptionally critical for ensuring the safety of Alphabet’s AI models, particularly as development becomes more advanced in areas like inferencing, agentic AI, and code generation, where users may be exposed to malicious code injection. Wiz could potentially be integrated across Alphabet’s AI platforms, particularly for its Gemini Robotics models that may require heightened security to ensure that the AI-enabled robots aren’t compromised.

Overall, I believe Alphabet is developing a leading ecosystem for AI and may quickly become a leader by scale over the coming years. Through its partnerships in deepening its custom silicon reach, internal AI development efforts, and secured ecosystem, I believe Alphabet’s market moat may be growing at an accelerating pace.

Alphabet Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to eq1’26, I’m leaving my initial forecast unchanged with $108.5b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $2.64. The change in my forecast runs from e2h26 through 2028, particularly driven by increased margin-accretive cloud revenue. I’m now forecasting Alphabet to achieve $200b in Google Cloud revenue in eFY28 underpinned by large-scale agreements for custom silicon and further development of its AI models.

Risks Related to Alphabet

Bull Case

Alphabet is gaining traction for third-party custom silicon sales, potentially expanding its footprint across data center environments beyond cloud services. Advancements in AI development could compound growth over the coming years, particularly for agentic AI, AI inferencing, and physical AI.

Bear Case

While Alphabet's Google Cloud operating margins have made significant improvements in recent quarters, they still lag peers like Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN). Anthropic's latest model, Mythos, has the potential to overshadow the Gemini & Wiz integration theme.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GOOG shares are currently trading at 26.37x EV/EBITDA, a rich valuation, though justified given the growth potential for Google Cloud. Fundamentally, I believe GOOG shares should be priced at $480/share at 19x eFY28 EV/EBITDA, underpinned by substantial growth in cloud and silicon solutions; I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" for GOOG shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Philip Morris: Growth At An Expensive Price (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) delivered an impressive set of financials for the first fiscal quarter of FY 2026 on Wednesday that showed that "ZYN" and "IQOS" products now represent a massive 43% of the tobacco firm’s consolidated revenues. Philip Morris has adapted successfully to changing consumer tastes and is shifting revenues to higher-margin products such as vapes and heated tobacco sticks... leading the enterprise to post 9.1% year-over-year top-line growth in Q1'26.

I like Philip Morris as a play on smoke-free (i.e., non-traditional) products, like vapes, but especially for its reliable dividend income. With the enterprise also confirming its FY 2026 EPS guidance and PM on track to pay out only ~75% of its adjusted earnings, I see no risks to the dividend and expect to see a continual uptrend in margins, led by new product categories. I am upgrading shares to hold given the firm's top line and especially gross profit growth, but I would wait for a larger drop before buying into the tobacco company.

Previous Rating

In July 2025 I rated Philip Morris International a ‘Sell’ -- Time To Take Profits -- primarily because shares were expensive at the time, trading north of 21X forward earnings. Despite those valuation concerns, Philip Morris' actual business is improving: the company posted strong organic revenue growth in the first quarter amid momentum, especially in the alternative products segment, though the combustible (legacy) tobacco business also saw positive growth. From a dividend safety and coverage perspective, I believe Philip Morris is a high-quality income investment, and given the firm's momentum, I am upgrading shares to hold.

Strong Gross Profit Growth Driven By Alternative Products

Philip Morris reported better-than-expected results for Q1'26, mainly because of momentum in the non-traditional tobacco segment: PM published $1.96 per share in adjusted earnings, which surpassed consensus estimates by $0.11 per share. In addition, top-line results crushed consensus revenue expectations by $240M.

Philip Morris benefited from impressive revenue growth on a Y/Y basis in Q1'26, kicking off the new fiscal year on a positive note: the tobacco company reported $10.1B in total net revenues (after excise taxes), showing a 9.1% year-over-year growth rate. This growth, as the breakdown will show later, was mainly achieved by alternative products, which now represented approximately 43% of the company's entire consolidated top line. At the current rate of growth, non-combustible products are set to account for more than half of all revenues in FY 2028, if not sooner.

Philip Morris benefited from solid momentum in its non-combustible, or non-traditional, business, which includes its IQOS-branded heated tobacco sticks and its oral nicotine pouches. Most importantly, while Philip Morris' alternative products grew a massive 15.8% Y/Y organically in Q1'26 (vs. 1.0% growth in the combustible segment), the firm's gross profits are growing at an even faster rate.

In the first quarter, Philip Morris' gross profits in the alternative products segment grew 19.4% Y/Y compared to just 3.9% Y/Y in the international combustible segment, highlighting a near-5X higher growth factor for PM's latest product innovations. Since PM is driving both its ZYN expansion globally and improving distribution of IQOS products, the company maintains a solid amount of growth momentum throughout the remainder of FY 2026.

Dividend Coverage And Growth Prospects

Philip Morris previously set its full-year 2026 EPS guidance at a range of $8.11-8.26 (excluding currency impacts), which the company today confirmed. Assuming that PM will raise its dividend by ~4% in September, which I consider likely given its strong Q1'26 financial performance, the tobacco company is on track to pay out approximately $6.00 per share in total dividends in 2026... implying an estimated forward dividend payout ratio of ~73%. The excess coverage margin here indicates that PM's dividend is highly secured by the firm's earnings power and is most likely going to grow for years to come.

Philip Morris' transition to non-traditional products has been highly effective so far, with business momentum clearly building on the back of global ZYN (oral nicotine pouches) expansion and growing IQOS distribution in the U.S. As a result, the enterprise has been able to grow its dividend faster than rivals like Altria (MO) and British American Tobacco (BTI). In the last three years, Philips Morris generated almost 16% (cumulative) growth in its base dividend, mainly because of last year's aggressive 8.9% dividend increase.

Philip Morris’ Valuation

For Philip Morris' higher-than-average earnings per share growth, which is driven by the tobacco company's focus on emerging markets, investors are paying a premium for its earnings potential. Shares of Philip Morris are currently trading at a forward, FY 2027, price-to-earnings ratio of 16.8X, which is only slightly above the historical valuation multiplier of 16.4X.

Industry peers like Altria and British American Tobacco trade at forward P/E ratios of 11.2X and 10.4X... mainly because the market expects PM to do better given its operational focus on less-saturated markets, while Altria, as an example, is mainly focused on the mature U.S. business.

Still, shares of Philip Morris are significantly more expensive than those of its rivals, and I would currently not start a new position in the tobacco and alternative products company unless I were to get at least a decent-sized discount to the firm's 3-year average P/E ratio. A, say, ~10% discount would be appealing to me... which would improve my margin of safety considerably: a valuation multiplier of 14.8X calculates to a potential buy point of $134 per share. I would also consider, at this price point, changing my rating on Philip Morris from 'Hold' to 'Buy.'

Primary Risks For PM Investors

Most investors are likely invested in shares of Philip Morris because of the company's potential to return more cash to investors. This capital return potential is linked to PM's strong profitability of its core combustible business as well as the growth potential in the alternative products segment. What would therefore get me to change my mind about Philip Morris' value proposition is if PM saw either a significant deceleration of gross profit growth in the alternative products category or weaker adjusted EPS growth going forward. The dividend for now, as far as I can tell, is not at risk for investors seeking an investment purely for income considerations.

Final Thoughts

Philip Morris is growing its alternative products business, driven by momentum in the IQOS brand as well as in the oral nicotine business. While there are long-term challenges to Philip Morris’ core tobacco business amid a shift away from cigarettes and towards vape products, the company is adapting well to changes in consumer behavior. While Philip Morris is growing its gross profits 5X faster than its combustible-related gross profits, I find shares still not all that appealing from a valuation point of view... and I would therefore wait for a drop towards ~$134 per share.

data.title: Warsh Advocates For Regime Change And Narrows The Definition Of Fed Independence
data.description:
Warsh Confirmation Hearing

At his Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing yesterday, Kevin Warsh finally had an opportunity to speak publicly about how he would lead the Fed for the first time since he was chosen in January as President Trump’s Fed Chair Nominee. In fact, it was Warsh’s first public comments in 5 months, following his November 2025 Wall Street Journal Opinion Article entitled “The Federal Reserve’s Broken Leadership.”

Warsh was questioned by the senators for over 2 ½ hours, and covered a wide variety of topics, ranging from the impact of AI on the economy, digital assets, affordability, the size of the Fed balance sheet and most importantly, Fed independence.

What we learned is that, when confirmed, Kevin Warsh will be a different type of Fed Chair.

Regime Change

From his first answer, Warsh was critical of the errors the Fed has made over the past several years. Focusing on inflation, he noted that post-covid “prices went up to the tune of 25% to 35% for virtually all deciles of Americans, and that is an indication that the Fed missed its mark. We are still dealing with the policy errors in 2021 and 2022. Once you let inflation take hold of the economy, it is more expensive and harder to bring down…We need fundamental policy reforms to fix it…that means a Regime Change in the conduct of policy.”

No, he is not looking for a change in personnel, but in how the Fed uses the tools at its disposal to make changes in monetary policy.

Warsh stated

“the Fed has an interest rate tool and a balance sheet tool, and my view is that the interest rate tool gets in the cracks, it’s fairer. The balance sheet tool, disproportionally, helps those with financial assets. The interest rate tool hits the entire economy. So we need a new framework, new tools.”

In addition, Warsh felt that the Fed’s current communication strategy is part of the reason for the policy errors.

“The Fed gives forward guidance. The Fed tells the whole world what the dots are going to be, what the forecasts are going to be. Well, the Fed is human. They hold onto those forecasts longer than they should. I think if the Fed were to wait until they get into a meeting before making decisions, that incremental deliberation can keep the central bank from compounding its error. I think these are the big changes that are needed, and if confirmed, I look forward to doing it.”

Independence

A large part of the hearing focused on independence.

A lingering question—amid President Trump’s public calls for lower interest rates and his attempts to influence the Fed’s leadership, including efforts to remove Lisa Cook and investigate Chair Powell over headquarters renovation cost overruns—is how independent Warsh would be from presidential efforts to shape Fed policy decisions.

Only hours before the confirmation hearing, President Trump gave an interview on CNBC where he said he would be disappointed if Warsh did not cut rates right away.

Warsh was very forceful in his statements during questioning that he would be independent. “I take my responsibility to be an independent leader of the Federal Reserve very seriously…. I will be an independent actor if confirmed.”

However, looking more closely, we can see evidence of how Warsh is aligning himself with the President, which is contrary to his claim of independence.

In his opening statement before the Senate Finance Committee Warsh’s view on independence is quite narrow. There he states, “Fed independence is at its peak in the operational conduct of monetary policy.”

But monetary policy is only one of the Fed’s five main responsibilities. The others are:

  • To promote the stability of the financial system to contain systemic risk.
  • To promote the safety and soundness of individual financial institutions.
  • To foster the efficiency and soundness of the payment and settlement system, and
  • To promote consumer protection and community development.

With regard to these, Warsh said “That degree of independence does not extend to the full range of its congressionally mandated functions.”

Further, concerning monetary policy independence, while Warsh says it is essential, he only addresses the Fed’s mandate for controlling inflation.

“Congress tasked the Fed with the mission to ensure price stability, without excuse or equivocation, argument or anguish. Inflation is a choice, and the Fed must take responsibility for it.”

His opening statement ignores any mention of the Fed’s second mandate of promoting maximum employment.

And, by narrowly defining Fed independence as he does, he can state

“I do not believe the operational independence of monetary policy is particularly threatened when elected officials-presidents, senators, or members of the House-state their views from all corners.”

Conveniently, by using his narrow definition of “operational independence of monetary policy” he allows the President to demand lower rates without infringing on the Fed’s independence.

This is a marked change from most mainstream definitions of Fed independence.

Warsh makes another concession to President Trump’s demand for Fed oversight when he states “Fed officials are not entitled to the same special deference in their stewardship of public monies.”

This statement alludes to the ongoing controversy over Federal Reserve renovation cost overruns, which has embroiled Chair Powell.

For the record, the Fed is a self-funding institution and does not rely on public funds. Being structured as an independent agency means it is independent in every sense. This includes paying for its own renovation costs.

Finally, Warsh adhered to the “Loyalty Test” the Trump administration gives to every political appointee. When questioned by Senator Warren if Donald Trump lost the 2020 presidential election, Warsh’s reply was “I believe this body certified that election many years ago.” He would not say that Trump lost in 2020, which is factually correct and was upheld in over 60 court cases.

Just this month a study by Demand Justice showed that all 40 of President Trump’s judicial nominees, when asked if Trump lost the 2020 election, would not answer yes. Instead, they referred to the certification process, as Warsh did.

New Measure For Inflation

Under questioning by Senator Cortez Masto about inflation, Warsh agreed that over the past 5 years inflation has exceeded the Fed’s 2% target, and that the Core PCE is currently around 3%, 1% above target.

When asked whether or not the tariffs imposed over the past year contributed to that excess in inflation, he pushed back.

Warsh said that

“the data being used on inflation is imperfect…the Fed needs to try and use our new understanding and new data sources to see what is really the inflation rate in the economy…I am interested in the underlying inflation rate, not what is the one-time change in prices because of a change in geopolitics…The measures I prefer are looking at the Trimmed Averages where we take out all of the tail risks, all of the one-off items and we ask ourselves where the generalized change in prices is having secondary effects on the economy.”

While acknowledging, this is not main stream, Warsh said many economists are looking at these new measures.

What Warsh was referring to is an inflation measure created by the Dallas Fed called the Trimmed Mean PCE. Introduced in 2005, the Trimmed Mean PCE inflation rate uses data from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. The individual prices changes are sorted in ascending order, and a certain percentage of the most extreme observations at both ends of the table are thrown out, or trimmed. The inflation rate is then calculated using the remaining components. By eliminating the tails, the index excludes one-time changes, and is a proxy for the Core PCE.

The chart below compares the Trimmed Mean PCE Inflation Rate to the Core PCE since 2000, when the Fed adopted PCE as its primary measure of inflation. While there is a strong correlation, Core PCE has been below Trimmed Mean PCE 75% of the time. More recently though, the trend has switched, with Core PCE exceeding Trimmed Mean. Over the past year there has been an atypical divergence. For the most recent data points, Core PCE was 3.0% while Trimmed Mean PCE came in at 2.3%.

The Dallas Fed just released a study that explained more than 50% of the overshoot in Core PCE is primarily tariff driven. Over the past year core goods prices, which were impacted by the tariffs, pushed up Core PCE, while Trimmed Mean viewed those as one-time items, and thus did not include them in the calculation.

Balance Sheet Reduction

Warsh was quite vocal in his view that the Fed should reduce the size of their balance sheet. During his prior term as a Fed Governor, he was supportive of balance sheet expansion, particularly during a crisis, but felt it was a tool that should be halted when the crisis passed. He left the Fed in 2011 because he opposed the continuation of balance sheet expansion through QE.

The Fed balance sheet totaled $800 billion when Warsh first joined the Fed and grew to $2.5 trillion when he resigned.

The Fed continued with QE, then tried to normalize for a short period before initiating QE again during the pandemic. Altogether, the Fed implemented QE over a 14-year period, and the balance sheet peaked at almost $9 trillion.

The Fed started to shrink their balance sheet beginning in 2022 when they implemented their Policy Normalization Plan. As part of their effort to fight the spike in inflation, the First raised rates, then began balance sheet reduction through maturity runoffs, more commonly known as Quantitative Tightening (QT).

For 3 ½ years, QT was in place and total assets fell by $2.4 trillion, a 27% decline. Then, in December 2025, the Fed determined that they had reached their desired level of ample reserves, and they ended QT.

Warsh thinks there is more room to go.

He is supported by his Stanford colleague, Darrell Duffie, Professor of Finance, who published a paper on how the Fed can shrink their balance sheet.

He is also supported by Fed Governor Stephen Moran, who also published “A User’s Guide to Reducing the Federal Reserve’s Balance Sheet.”

Both focus on how to reduce the demand for bank reserves.

Warsh though, is concerned about the asset side of the balance sheet as well. He believes that the Fed should not own long term Treasury bonds or Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS).

While he doesn’t put a level on how small the balance sheet should be, he wants to get started.

An issue he will be confronted with, is one that the current Fed also had to address.

The securities Warsh wants to eliminate were purchased when interest rates were much lower, and consequently have a market value below their current carrying cost. As of year-end, the Fed’s Treasury holdings had an unrealized loss of $535.3 billion, while their MBS holdings had an unrealized loss of $309 billion. The combined $844 billion unrealized loss represents a 13% reduction from cost.

The fastest way pare their holdings is through a sale, but if the securities were sold, it would force the Fed to record the huge losses. This is something they are unlikely to do. In addition, Warsh is sensitive to the market impact such a large sale would have. He recognizes it would put upward pressure on bond rates, and he doesn’t want to disrupt the market. As such, any reduction would have to be slow and gradual. He stated “it took 18 years to create this balance sheet problem, we won’t be able to fix it in 18 minutes.”

Change In Communication

One of the key innovations implemented by Former Fed Chair Ben Bernanke was the concept of steering the Fed to be more transparent. He instituted holding regular press conferences to better explain monetary policy decisions and to provide forward guidance. He believed in allowing investors to have a better understanding of the direction of Fed policy.

It seems that Warsh wants to walk that back.

While he was noncommittal as to a schedule, he seemed to indicate it wasn’t necessary to have Fed officials speak so frequently, and he felt the forward guidance was detrimental to policy decisions, as it locked governors into their positions, whether intentionally, or not.

He seemed to be advocating for less transparency. This would be a reversion to the days of Alan Greenspan, who preceded Bernanke as Fed Chair. Greenspan was the master of obfuscation who famously said, “I know you think you understand what you thought I said, but I’m not sure you realize that what you heard is not what I meant.”

If that is the case, it would be a return to the period of a lot of guessing as to what Fed policy is.

Obstacles To The Warsh Confirmation

Even though Warsh is done with the Senate Banking Committee Confirmation Hearing, his path to confirmation is not smooth.

Senator Tillis reiterated that he will not support the nomination while the case against Chair Powell is still outstanding. The DOJ and President Trump appear to want to keep moving forward with the Fed investigation.

That leaves things up in the air.

Polymarket has a 1% chance that Warsh will be confirmed by May 1st, and only a 30% chance that he will be in place when Powell’s term expires on May 15th. They are more optimistic, though, with a 91% chance of confirmation by June 30th.

As a side note, Warsh revealed an investment position in Polymarket when he filed his financial disclosure forms with the Office of Government Ethics.

Is it a conflict of interest that people are betting on him on the Polymarket platform in which he holds a financial stake?

Conclusion

Yesterday Kevin Warsh spent more than 2 ½ hours testifying before the Senate Banking committee in his confirmation hearing to become the new Fed Chair.

For the first time since his nomination in January, Warsh was able to express his views on how he sees performing his responsibilities if he is confirmed as Fed Chair.

Warsh wants to do things differently, as he cited having a Regime Change, which he termed as a new direction in which to take the Fed.

He plans to change the communications format currently in place, as he sees forward guidance as being too restrictive. He also wants to reduce the frequency of public comments.

From a monetary policy perspective, Warsh wants to sharpen the tools the Fed uses to achieve their mandate. He wants to emphasize their primary tool of interest rate management, which he believes “fills the cracks” and impacts all Americans, while he wants to reduce the size of the Fed’s balance sheet, which he feels only benefits those who own assets.

While this is an admirable goal, Warsh wasn’t able to articulate how he planned on achieving his objective, and he didn’t address the many impediments to doing so.

His only comment was that he would proceed slowly and thoughtfully.

The major point of contention was over Fed independence, and while Warsh reiterated over and over that he was committed to independence, and that he took it seriously, several of his actions belied that commitment.

First, he narrowed his definition of Fed independence to include only “operational monetary policy” and stated “That degree of independence does not extend to the full range of its congressionally mandated functions.”

Additionally, he stated that

“I do not believe the operational independence of monetary policy is particularly threatened when elected officials-presidents, senators, or members of the House-state their views from all corners.”

This allows him to say he is independent and still have President Trump dictate that the Fed should cut rates.

Finally, he showed an alliance with the president by adhering to the loyalty test of not stating the fact that Trump lost the 2020 election.

Inflation is a priority, as stated by the Fed mandate, but Warsh wants to find a better way to measure the impact of changes in prices.

He mentioned the Trimmed Mean PCE Inflation Rate compiled by the Dallas Fed, instead of using the Fed’s current Core PCE.

While I support an accurate way of measuring prices, Trimmed Mean has existed for the duration since the Fed moved to PCE, and I wonder if the fact that his attention is now focused on Trimmed Mean because it is below Core PCE is the reason.

With all of this, we are still up in the air about confirmation because of the standoff between Senator Tillis and the DOJ investigation into Chair Powell. How that is resolved is still unknown.

Polymarket is only giving a 30% chance of confirmation by May 15th.

Stay tuned!

data.title: Kyverna Therapeutics: 'Strong Buy' On Additional POC Of Miv-Cel In Myasthenia Gravis
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Kyverna Therapeutics (KYTX), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Kyverna Therapeutics: "Strong Buy" Rating As Positive SPS Data Leads To 1st Half 2026 BLA Filing." With respect to this article, I mentioned that this company had achieved the primary endpoint of its single-arm registrational phase 2 KYSA-8 study using miv-cel for the treatment of patients with stiff person syndrome [SPS]. Specifically, this therapy allowed it to meet the primary endpoint of the change from baseline in the timed 25-foot walk [T25FW] at 16 weeks in a statistically significant manner. At that time, I had a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock because of this feat that was achieved.

It has also previously released positive data from its phase 2 portion of its phase 2/3 KYSA-6 registrational study using miv-cel for the treatment of patients with Generalized Myasthenia Gravis [gMG]. This was an interim analysis of 6 patients with gMG who were given miv-cel. It was noted that 100% [6 out of 6] of patients who took this therapy were able to achieve clinically meaningful reductions in MG-ADL and QMG scores from baseline regardless of prior biologic experience. Today, I'm going to maintain a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock. There are several reasons why I believe such a rating should remain. The first of which is because the company just released a late-breaking oral presentation of data from the KYSA-8 trial.

The point is that the company is well equipped to file its Biologics License Application [BLA] of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with stiff person syndrome [SPS] in the 1st half of 2026. This sets the ability for its autologous CD-19 CAR T-cell therapy, miv-cel, to become the first treatment option for patients with SPS. The second reason is because the company also presented long-term data showing that miv-cel was able to achieve deepening of responses in the ongoing KYSA-6 trial out to 52 weeks. I will be going over this specific program below because of the newly updated data. The final reason is because of miv-cel's ability to achieve deep B-cell depletion and immune reset, it holds potential across other autoimmune disorders.

There are two investigator-initiated trials [IITs] exploring the use of this CAR-T cell therapy in the targeting of patients with progressive multiple sclerosis [PMS] and rheumatoid arthritis [RA]. Positive data has been released from both of these early-stage programs already. However, there is an expectation of additional data to be released from the phase 2 portion of the phase 1/2 IIT study using miv-cel in difficult-to-treat RA patients in 2026. With an expected BLA filing of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS in the 1st half of 2026, plus continued deepening of response observed using the same autologous CD-19 CAR T-cell therapy treating patients with gMG, I believe that this stock should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Deepening Of Responses In Myasthenia Gravis Bodes Well For Ongoing Registrational KYSA-6 Study

As I mentioned above, Kyverna Therapeutics is doing very well in terms of its clinical advancement of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with stiff person syndrome [SPS]. This is in terms of it being able to achieve the primary endpoint of its phase 2 registrational KYSA-8 study using this therapy to treat these patients. It was able to present primary analysis data as part of an oral late-breaking presentation at the 2026 American Academy of Neurology [AAN] meeting on April 21, 2026. The company is on track to submit a BLA filing of miv-cel to the FDA for the treatment of these SPS patients in the 1st half of 2026. This sets up a major inflection point for investors to keep an eye on. However, for the purpose of this article, I'm going to go over the other indication that Kyverna is going after with miv-cel, which is Generalized Myasthenia Gravis [gMG]. The use of this autologous CD19-targeting CAR T-cell therapy is being evaluated to target this specific B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorder in the ongoing phase 2/3 KYSA-6 trial. I will also be going over other additional expansion opportunities, because the beauty of miv-cel is the ability to go after other B-cell-mediated disorders. Before going over the newly released KYSA-6 trial data, plus any catalysts that investors should keep an eye on, I believe that it is first important to go over what Generalized Myasthenia Gravis is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Myasthenia Gravis [MG] is characterized as a chronic autoimmune neuromuscular disorder whereby there is an effect on the voluntary muscles of the patient's body. What makes this disorder devastating is that it just doesn't only affect one aspect of a person's body; it instead goes after a variety of items. Such items it goes after are:

  • Weakness in the arms and legs
  • Difficulty swallowing
  • Droopy eyelids
  • Breathing problems
  • Being able to speak

What exactly causes all of these problems? Well, MG is caused because there is a breakdown of signaling between the nerves and muscles of the body. Furthermore, this breakdown is caused by autoantibodies that are specifically produced by B-cells. If this is the case, then how can a therapy like miv-cel help? The reason why is because this CAR T-cell therapy is able to specifically deplete B-cells in the person's body by targeting the surface protein of CD19. In turn, autoreactive B-cells no longer occur, and this immune reset results in no disruption of signaling between the nerves and muscles. In addition, it is important to mention that MG has another form. MG is considered to be the overall disease type, where there are milder symptoms that deal with the eye and/or throat only. The more widespread version of MG, known as Generalized Myasthenia Gravis [gMG] involves arms, legs, respiratory muscles, and other aspects. The main advantage for miv-cel is that it offers complete B-cell depletion, while other therapies, like mRNA CAR-T, antibodies, and T-cell engagers, do not. There are two main issues with current available therapies for gMG, which are [Kyverna Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #30]:

  • Few of them reaching minimal system expression [MSE] for a patient
  • The need for ongoing chronic immunosuppressant therapy

As I will be showing below in the clinical data that the company released, the KYSA-6 registrational trial was able to get rid of chronic immunosuppressant therapy for 24-weeks after a single dose of miv-cel. There is definitely a market opportunity here for Kyverna Therapeutics. That's because the global Generalized Myasthenia Gravis market is expected to reach $11.9 billion by 2034. This is a very large market, and the key item to point out is that the SPS is just a stepping stone over to gMG, as they are both part of the company's neuroimmunology franchise [Kyverna Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #9]. The SPS centers to be laid out, if miv-cel is given FDA approval for it, will allow the company to transition over to treating patients with gMG.

In order to see whether or not miv-cel would be adequate enough to help treat patients with gMG, the company is in the process of running the ongoing phase 2/3 KYSA-6 study. The thing is that the company had released interim data already from this particular study, and this was in 6 patients with a cut-off evaluation date of October 3, 2025. It was noted that 100% [6 out of 6] of patients given a single dose of 1×10^8 CAR+T cells miv-cel achieved clinically meaningful reductions of MG-ADL and QMG scores from baseline of -8.0 points and -7.7 points at 24 weeks. This also was the first instance in which it was noted that all of these patients during this period of time were able to forgo their treatments of:

  • Nonsteroidal immunosuppressants
  • High-dose steroids >10 mg
  • FcRn inhibitors
  • Complement inhibitors

This was the last time around; however, the company just presented new data from the phase 2 portion of the phase 2/3 KYSA-6 study using miv-cel to treat these patients with gMG. What makes this new data significant? Well, it did add one other patient for starters. Thus, with the data update, there were 7 patients who were evaluated for MG-ADL and QMG scores. Secondly, the main item to note is that deep B-cell depletion was able to result in deep responses for an extended period of time.

With a cut-off date of February 25, 2026, it was noted that 7 patients with moderate-to-severe gMG given a single dose of 1X10^8 CAR+T cells of miv-cel achieved mean reductions in MG-ADL and QMG scores of -8.5 points and -11.3 points at week 24, respectively. Plus, like last time, it was noted that all patients were free of chronic immunosuppressant therapy as well. What's highly important in the newly updated data are two things. First, the responses that were achieved were sustained out to 52 weeks. However, this was only established in the three evaluable patients who were followed-up for that period of time. The second of which involves biomarker data, which lends credibility to the deep response observed over the extended period of time of 52 weeks. What do I mean by this? Well, the mechanism of action [MOA] ended up working out as intended, because there was a complete immune reset observed. That is, the expansion of CAR T-cells achieved resulted in deep B-cell depletion for all the patients who were treated. Remember above? Where I state that a lot of patients don't end up achieving MSE? Well, this is what came into play with this data, where 57% of patients achieved MSE, which is defined as having an MG-ADL score of 0 or 1. This proves the therapy has the ability to help these patients in a superior manner compared to currently approved therapies for gMG, along with an excellent safety profile. Speaking of safety, the data revealed that there were no high-grade cytokine-release syndrome [CRS] or immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome [ICANS] either.

The main thing to state is that the data achieved in the phase 2 portion of the KYSA-6 trial was highly ideal and also bodes well for the ongoing phase 3 study. This is important because the phase 3 study is evaluating miv-cel for the treatment of these gMG patients based on both co-primary endpoints of MG-ADL and QMG scores. This phase 2/3 study was amended in 2025 to be a registrational one, and the hope is that the final outcome can be replicated. That doesn't mean that there isn't another catalyst to consider in 2026, beyond that of the expected BLA filing of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS, expected in the 1st half of 2026. The beauty of the MOA of deep B-cell depletion is that it can be applied to a variety of other autoimmune disorders. One such program would be the ongoing phase 1/2 IIT evaluating the use of this autologous CD19-targeting CAR T-cell therapy for difficult-to-treat RA patients who have failed with multiple prior therapies. The point here is that the company is expected to release data from the phase 2 portion of this trial in 2026. Prior phase 1 data pointed to 4 out of 6 patients achieving an ACR20 response [20% improvement criteria], with 2 of these responders achieving ACR50. The point here is that these are difficult-to-treat patients because they have treatment-resistant RA. Lastly, other milestones would be data readouts of miv-cel-treated patients with lupus nephritis [LN] and progressive multiple sclerosis [PMS].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, Kyverna Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $279.3 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company had enacted a public offering back on December 17, 2025, for a total of $100 million for starters. Not only that, but the company also had to obtain funding with respect to a Loan and Security Agreement with Oxford Finance LLC for an aggregate of $150 million. The way this loan is structured is as follows:

  • One tranche of $40 million initially
  • Two tranches of $30 million [total $60 million]
  • Fourth tranche of $50 million

However, on November 3, 2025, it only drew $25 million from the first tranche under this loan agreement. The company is in excellent shape in terms of its cash on hand. Why do I state that? That's because in its Q4 and full-year 202 financial results update, it mentions that it believes its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations into 2028. The company burns about $39.3 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $30 million for its R&D expenses and then $9.3 million for its G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Kyverna Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the expected BLA filing of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS. The risk here is there is no assurance that the FDA is going to accept the BLA of this CAR T-cell therapy for review of this neuroimmunology indication. Even if the FDA does accept the review of this drug, there can be no guarantee that U.S. marketing approval of it will happen. Especially in light of what has been going on with the FDA lately. There is a problem when it comes to obtaining approval on single-arm, open-label studies. Thus, because the KYSA-8 trial was not done in a randomized trial, this might potentially become an issue. For example, there was hope that uniQure (QURE) would be able to obtain FDA approval of its gene therapy AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with Huntington's Disease based on a single-arm study being compared to an external control. However, the FDA rejected it. Not only that, but meeting minutes from an FDA Type A meeting noted that the company must run a randomized, double-blind, sham surgery-controlled study. The point is that this presents itself as a risk because there is no assurance that it will be able to obtain U.S. marketing approval of miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS based on a single-arm, open-label trial.

The second risk to consider would be that of the other indication that miv-cel is being evaluated for, which is the treatment of patients with gMG in the ongoing phase 2/3 KYSA-6 registrational trial. As I have noted above, in the latest updated data from the phase 2 portion of this study, patients who took miv-cel achieved mean reductions in MG-ADL and QMG scores of -8.5 points and -11.3 points at week 24, respectively. This bodes well for the ongoing phase 3 study, because it utilizes these same co-primary endpoints. The risk here is that there is no assurance that these endpoints are going to be met in the ongoing phase 3 portion of this phase 2/3 KSYA-6 registrational trial for starters. In essence, there is no guarantee that the data generated thus far in these 7 patients will be reproduced in a larger pool of patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunities for Kyverna Therapeutics. The reason why is because the hope of the company is that miv-cel could become a "pipeline in a product," which is to be able to be applicable towards other B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders. With that said, as I mentioned above, it is in the process of exploring this autologous CD19-targeting CAR T-cell therapy in two ongoing ITTs targeting patients with PMS and difficult-to-treat RA. There is an expectation of data updates from both of these trials to happen during 2026. The risk here is that even though miv-cel has been shown to work well in being able to treat patients with SPS and gMG, there is no assurance that it will be able to continue to deliver solid clinical data in both of these IITs.

Conclusion

Kyverna Therapeutics has done well to continue to develop the use of its autologous CD19-targeting CAR T-cell therapy miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS. As I have gone over above, it already has reported positive topline data from its phase 2 KYSA-8 trial using this therapy to treat patients with SPS. The beauty of this is that it sets up a BLA filing of miv-cel for the treatment of these patients in the 1st half of 2026.

This program and proof of concept [POC] of miv-cel to achieve complete B-cell depletion, along with an immune reset, is further bolstered by long-term [52-week] follow-up data from the phase 2 portion of the phase 2/3 KYSA-6 trial targeting patients with gMG. Based on the ability to file a BLA in the 1st half of 2026 for miv-cel for the treatment of patients with SPS, plus the ability to expand to other B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders, like gMG, PMS, and RA, I believe that it is important to maintain a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock.

data.title: Ben Carlson: Long Term Investing Still Wins - Even When It Feels Wrong
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Portfolio Manager, Author, Podcaster Ben Carlson on why markets are fascinating (0:30) Private credit, banking sectors (4:40) Market cycles speeding up (8:00) Economy vs stock market (12:20) Gold and safe havens (22:50) Dividend stocks, yield, income investing and ETFs (24:30) Earnings season: Listen to how CEOs talk about consumers (28:00) AI evolution (31:20)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome to Investing Experts, Mr. Ben Carlson. I'm sure many of you have heard him or heard of him at the very least. You are with Ritholtz Wealth Management. You manage institutions there. You have a very fabulous podcast, the Animal Spirits Podcast. You are an author too.

To wit, we are here today for the most part to talk about your newest book, Risk and Reward: How to handle market volatility and build long-term wealth. Really, really happy to have you on the show. Been listening to you, reading you for a long time. So thanks for coming on the show.

Ben Carlson: Thanks for having me.

Rena Sherbill: Talk to us. Maybe I'd be interested to hear first off, if you could share with listeners how you spend your day, how you spend your day looking at the markets, understanding them, how you digest them and then what led you to write this specific book at this specific moment.

Ben Carlson: I start my day reading the horoscope just to make sure I know what's going on there. Line of the stars. I think I do a lot of writing and the best way for me to do that is by doing a lot of reading. So I'm doing a lot of reading about what's going on. I pay attention to a lot of numbers and data.

And I personally think the markets are just fascinating. I know that there's some people outside of finance who think like this stuff is just it's boring numbers mumbo jumbo. I think that like the interplay between numbers and feelings and emotions in human psychology.

I think that the markets are just this like giant laboratory for studying human beings. I think it's like one of the best places to look at the different emotions that human beings have. Fear, greed, panic, euphoria, all these different things that the markets could bring about.

And so I really enjoy just following the market. So that's why like talking about them. I like writing about them.

Rena Sherbill: Why you called your podcast Animal Spirits, perhaps.

Ben Carlson: Yeah. On a daily basis, that's a lot of what I'm doing. I'm talking to our financial advisors at my wealth management firm. I'm talking to clients to get a better understanding of what they're doing.

I'm creating content. And actually, a lot of that stuff, dealing with clients and hearing their concerns and worries and what the problems they're trying to solve, that's really good for me in terms of producing content because that's the stuff that I'm trying to think about.

What are people actually worried about these days? are like regular people outside of finance? What are they worried about?

So I've been writing my blog for a little over 10 years now. And the whole point of me writing a blog in the first place, I kind of got into a little trepidation. was right when financial blogs were kind of taking off in like the early 2010s.

So I was reading Josh Brown and Barry Ritholtz, who I'm now working with. They were some of the early blog people. And I just thought that there was a lot of negativity in the world coming out of the great financial crisis. And there was a lot of pessimism and I guess rightly so in a lot of ways because we had two huge stock market crashes and two recessions in the span of 10 years. There was a lost decade for the stock market.

People were really nervous, like, oh my gosh, the financial system almost ended. All these 100-plus-year-old firms went out of business, and the government is backstopping and saving places. I think there was a lot of people who just lacked faith and trust in the financial system. I was getting all these questions from my friends and family about, you're the finance guy. Explain this to us. What's going on here? That's why I started writing my blog.

I'm always kind of glass half full kind of guy. I look for the more optimistic and I look for the good side in most things. And I just thought that there's a lot of pessimism. That was the idea for the blog.

The book is, I've received a lot of pushback over the years. There's a lot of people who've taken on like this whole idea of like long-term investing and thinking and acting for the long term. But I get all these people who look for exceptions. Well, what about this? Well, what do you think about this? Wasn't this a terrible experience?

And I think for a lot of people, the whole idea of long-term investing is just it doesn't make sense in this world. And I'm trying to prove that no, even if we open the kimono and show all the bad stuff, right? Like, let's play devil's advocate to my own investing philosophy.

I'm to go through point by point and show everything bad that's happened in last 100 years and why this form of investing still makes sense. And so that was the idea just to, I look at like the risk and reward as like the yin and yang. I say that they're attached to the hip. That's what I wanted to show that like, despite all the nasty risks out there, like the reward is still worth it for long-term investors.

Rena Sherbill: We've been talking a lot recently on this podcast about the private credit sector and how it's coming up against the banking sector. And you just talked about the great financial crisis. You talk about it in the book also, a lot of comparisons being made to what's happening in the private credit sector to the great financial crisis.

We had Samuel Smith on talking last week about how that very much is not the case. He's a big bullish guy on Blue Owl (OWL) specifically, and he was laying the case for why the banking establishment or banking institutions or those that run banking institutions are so down on the private credit space.

Any thoughts to share about that discussion and also I guess, bear markets and great big bear markets and where bearishness has you most worried?

Ben Carlson: It is interesting that the whole private credit space seems to be an outcropping of the financial crisis, right? A lot of the banks pulled back from that type of lending, so the private managers stepped in, and now they're doing it.

I think the biggest difference between what happened in 2008 and now is just these loans are long, these loans are not, it's not like an event, it's more of a process. Let's say that the people who are worried about the credit quality of these loans, and I can't really speak to the credit quality, because that's just not my expertise.

And these loans are a little harder to understand, right? But let's say that the credit quality does go bad. It's not like these things on one day are all going to go under, right? And all these companies are going go bankrupt.

It would be more like a death by a thousand cuts. So that's where I think the analogy goes. Even if you thought the worst of these investments. I tend to think that these private managers have so much money and they have so much incentive to make sure that this stuff works out.

It's hard to see this being this sort of car crash scenario. That's kind of where I fall on it.
